notes.note: L. Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus, consul 58 BC, father-in-law of Julius Caesar; the consul of Cicero's exile-year, here returned from his Macedonian governorship and now in the Senate.
Of the Insubres, a Gallic people of Cisalpine Gaul (around modern Milan); Cicero's invective traces Piso's maternal grandfather to Placentia (modern Piacenza), an Insubrian colony.

The lex Aelia and lex Fufia, two laws of c. 150 BC restricting popular legislation by religious obstruction (\textit{obnuntiatio}); abolished by Clodius's tribunate in 58 BC. The 'bulwarks of tranquillity' for Cicero.
P. Clodius Pulcher, tribune of the plebs 58 BC, who carried Cicero's exile. The 'wicked debauchery, civil bloodshed' refer to the Bona Dea scandal of 62 BC.

The lex Iulia de rebus repetundis (Caesar's extortion law of 59 BC), establishing detailed regulations of provincial governors. Cicero contrasts Caesar's law (which freed peoples) with the consular-province law of Clodius (which gave them to Piso).
Cicero plays on the Cisalpine Gallic origin of Piso's mother — \textit{bracatae}, 'wearing trousers', a distinctively Gallic article of clothing in Roman eyes.
Pompey's three triumphs: African (81 BC, over the Marians), Spanish (71 BC, over Sertorius), and Asian (61 BC, over Mithridates). The most-recorded triple-triumph in Roman history before Caesar.

Mediolanum (modern Milan), in Cisalpine Gaul; Piso's maternal grandfather Calventius is sneered at as a praeco (auctioneer/crier) in this provincial town — a low-status occupation despised in Roman society.

notes.anchor: Greeks crammed five to a couch
notes.note: A breach of Roman triclinium etiquette, which had three couches with three diners each (totalling nine, the Muse-number). To pack five on a couch is the mark of a low Greek dive.

Philodemus of Gadara (c. 110-c. 35 BC), Epicurean philosopher and erotic poet, who lived in Piso's household; many of his epigrams survive in the Greek Anthology, and his prose-works are preserved on the carbonised papyri of the Villa of the Papyri at Herculaneum.

Cicero's most famous self-quotation, from his lost epic \textit{De Consulatu Suo} (c. 60 BC): 'Let arms yield to the toga, let the laurel yield to praise' — a much-mocked verse for its self-praise (cf. Sen. \textit{Suas.} 6.26).
Cotys, king of the Sapaean Thracians; Piso took 300 talents from him for the murder of Rabocentus, chief of the rival Bessi.

Probably the lex Pompeia of 55 BC reforming the album of judges by tribe and rank — making it harder for prosecutors to be packed against. Cicero hopes it will deliver fairer juries for any future trial of Piso.

P. Rutilius Rufus, consul 105 BC; condemned in 92 BC for repetundae in his governorship of Asia, despite (or because of) his austere honesty — which threatened the publicans on the equestrian juries. The locus classicus for the failure of provincial-governor courts under equestrian juries.
L. Opimius, consul 121 BC, the killer of Gaius Gracchus; condemned in 109 BC and exiled, dying at Dyrrachium. The locus classicus for the senate-versus-courts opposition.
